text: The surgeon retracts the colon and omentum superiorly to expose the duodenal jejunal flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein and artery are clearly visible, confirming the location of the flexure.

The surgeon assesses the bowel to determine which segment can be freely brought to the anterior wall of the stomach. The stomach appears dilated due to gastric outlet obstruction. The surgeon prepares to perform a gastrotomy using a harmonic scalpel, noting the thickness of the stomach wall.

The surgeon opens the gastrotomy using medallion forceps and suctions the stomach contents. The jejunum is brought up to the site of anastomosis after confirming no adhesions. The mesentery of the jejunum is opened, and an enterotomy is created using a monopolar hook, widened with Maryland forceps, revealing the mucosa. An endo-GIA stapler with a 60 mm blue cartridge is applied first to the jejunal side, then the gastric side, ensuring no omentum or mesentery interferes with the staple line before firing the stapler.

The surgeon completes the anterior gastrojejunostomy and assesses the staple line for any bleeding, suctioning out residue due to the gastric outlet obstruction. A 2-0 PDS suture is used to place a stay suture on the right side of the enterotomy edge, aiding in the completion of the anastomosis.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture thread long and begins continuous closure from the left of the enterotomy opening. The first bite is taken on the gastric side, followed by a reverse bite on the jejunal side, ensuring full thickness bites. The closure progresses from stomach to jejunum, with the surgeon tightening the sutures and pushing in any protruding mucosa for proper closure. The stay suture is utilized to expose angles for easier access, ensuring a tight closure with careful attention to the angles.

The surgeon reaches the edge of the anastomosis, going beyond the stay suture, and places the final knot to complete the gastrojejunostomy. An air leak test is performed to assess the adequacy of the closure, marking the completion of the procedure.
